gh-w3-f1.answer:
Create your first custom class — Student — to model real-world entities with fields and behavior.

Requirements:
1. Create Student.java with fields: int rollNumber, String name, int age, String branch, double percentage.
2. Add a constructor: Student(int rollNumber, String name, int age, String branch, double percentage) that assigns all fields using this.field = parameter.
3. Add methods:
   • void display() — prints all student details formatted.
   • boolean isPass() — returns true if percentage >= 40.
   • String getGrade() — returns A/B/C/D/F based on percentage (same scale as Week 2).
4. Create StudentDemo.java (or main in same file) that creates 2–3 Student objects and calls display(), isPass(), getGrade().

Key concepts:
• Class = blueprint; object = instance created with new Student(...).
• Fields store state; methods define behavior.
• this. refers to the current object's field when parameter names match.
• Encapsulation starts here — later you'll make fields private with getters/setters.
• One public class per file; filename must match class name.
gh-w3-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: Every enterprise app models entities — User, Order, Product — as classes. Student is the classic first OOP example because it maps directly to database rows and REST API JSON objects.

Compile & run:
javac Student.java StudentDemo.java
java StudentDemo

Push to java-week-3-oop:
git add Student.java StudentDemo.java
git commit -m "Add Student.java — first custom class with methods"
git push

Tip: If you get "cannot find symbol Student", compile both files — javac Student.java StudentDemo.java.

gh-w3-f2.answer:
Model an Employee with salary logic — practice constructors, fields, and computed methods.

Requirements:
1. Create Employee.java with fields: int id, String name, String department, double basicSalary.
2. Constructor to initialize all fields.
3. Methods:
   • double calculateHRA() — return 10% of basicSalary.
   • double calculateDA() — return 5% of basicSalary.
   • double calculateGrossSalary() — basic + HRA + DA.
   • void displayPayslip() — print id, name, department, basic, HRA, DA, gross.
4. In main/demo: create at least 2 employees in different departments, print payslips.

Key concepts:
• Methods can call other methods: calculateGrossSalary() uses calculateHRA() and calculateDA().
• Business rules live inside the class — gross salary formula is Employee's responsibility.
• double for money; printf %.2f for currency display.
• Separate data (fields) from calculations (methods) — same pattern as real payroll systems.
gh-w3-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: HRMS and payroll systems have Employee classes with exactly this structure. Gross/net pay calculations, tax deductions, and payslip generation are core enterprise features.

Compile & run:
javac Employee.java
java Employee

Test: basicSalary 50000 → HRA 5000, DA 2500, gross 57500.

Push to java-week-3-oop:
git add Employee.java
git commit -m "Add Employee.java — salary calculations"
git push

gh-w3-f3.answer:
Build a Car class — demonstrate object state, behavior, and simple validation.

Requirements:
1. Create Car.java with fields: String brand, String model, int year, double price, boolean isRunning (default false).
2. Constructor: Car(String brand, String model, int year, double price).
3. Methods:
   • void start() — if not running, set isRunning true and print "{brand} started".
   • void stop() — if running, set false and print stopped message.
   • void displayInfo() — print all car details including running status.
   • double depreciatedValue() — cars lose 15% per year from current year (2026): price * Math.pow(0.85, yearsOld).
4. Demo: create 2 cars, start/stop one, show depreciated value.

Key concepts:
• Object state changes over time (isRunning toggles).
• Methods guard state: don't start if already running.
• Math.pow(base, exponent) for depreciation formula.
• Objects are independent — starting car1 doesn't affect car2.
gh-w3-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: Vehicle fleet apps, rental platforms (Zoomcar, Uber), and dealership CRMs model cars as objects with status (available, rented, maintenance). State-changing methods mirror real lifecycle management.

Compile & run:
javac Car.java
java Car

Push to java-week-3-oop:
git add Car.java
git commit -m "Add Car.java — object state and behavior"
git push

gh-w3-f4.answer:
BankAccount class — encapsulation with deposit, withdraw, and balance tracking.

Requirements:
1. Create BankAccount.java with fields: String accountNumber, String holderName, double balance.
2. Constructor: initialize accountNumber, holderName, and opening balance (must be >= 0).
3. Methods:
   • void deposit(double amount) — reject if amount <= 0, else add and print confirmation.
   • void withdraw(double amount) — reject if amount <= 0 or amount > balance, else subtract.
   • double getBalance() — return current balance.
   • void displayAccount() — print account number, holder, balance.
4. Demo in main: create account, deposit 5000, withdraw 2000, try overdraft (should fail).

Key concepts:
• Encapsulation: balance should only change through deposit/withdraw — not direct assignment from outside.
• Validation inside methods protects object integrity.
• getBalance() is a getter — read-only access to private state (make fields private when ready).
• This class is the building block for BankManagementSystem in Week 4.
gh-w3-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: Core banking, UPI wallets, and fintech apps center on account objects with controlled money movement. Never expose raw balance for direct modification — always use methods with validation.

Compile & run:
javac BankAccount.java
java BankAccount

Push to java-week-3-oop:
git add BankAccount.java
git commit -m "Add BankAccount.java — deposit, withdraw, balance"
git push

Upgrade path: change fields to private and add proper getters — full encapsulation.

gh-w3-f5.answer:
Product class for inventory — fields, constructor, discount pricing, and stock display.

Requirements:
1. Create Product.java with fields: int id, String name, String category, double price, int quantity.
2. Constructor initializing all fields.
3. Methods:
   • double getDiscountedPrice(double discountPercent) — return price after discount (validate 0–100).
   • double getTotalValue() — price * quantity (inventory value).
   • void displayProduct() — print all details including total value.
   • void restock(int amount) — add to quantity if amount > 0.
   • boolean sell(int amount) — if quantity sufficient, reduce and return true; else false.
4. Demo: create products, sell some units, restock, show discounted price.

Key concepts:
• Inventory management: quantity goes up (restock) and down (sell).
• sell() returns boolean so caller knows if transaction succeeded.
• getTotalValue() is derived data — computed from price × quantity, not stored separately.
gh-w3-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart), warehouse systems, and POS terminals all use Product/InventoryItem classes. Stock checks before sale prevent overselling — same as your sell() method.

Compile & run:
javac Product.java
java Product

Push to java-week-3-oop:
git add Product.java
git commit -m "Add Product.java — inventory and discount pricing"
git push

gh-w3-f6.answer:
Library class with Book inner concept — manage a collection of books using arrays and objects.

Requirements:
1. Create Book.java: fields String title, String author, String isbn; boolean isAvailable (default true). Constructor + display().
2. Create Library.java: field Book[] books (fixed size, e.g., 50), int bookCount = 0.
3. Library methods:
   • void addBook(Book b) — if space available, add and increment bookCount.
   • void displayAllBooks() — loop and print each book.
   • void searchByTitle(String title) — find and display matching books.
   • boolean issueBook(String isbn) — set isAvailable false if found and available.
   • boolean returnBook(String isbn) — set isAvailable true.
4. Demo in main: add 3 books, display, issue one, search, return.

Key concepts:
• Composition: Library HAS-A collection of Book objects.
• Object arrays: Book[] books = new Book[50].
• bookCount tracks how many slots are filled — separate from array length.
• Search = loop through array comparing fields.
gh-w3-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: Library systems, catalog management, and any "collection of entities" app use this pattern. Arrays of objects evolve into ArrayList and databases in later weeks.

Compile & run:
javac Book.java Library.java
java Library

Push to java-week-3-oop:
git add Book.java Library.java
git commit -m "Add Library.java — book collection management"
git push

gh-w3-f7.answer:
Vehicle class hierarchy starter — base Vehicle before full inheritance in Week 4.

Requirements:
1. Create Vehicle.java with fields: String registrationNumber, String brand, double basePrice.
2. Constructor and displayInfo().
3. Method double calculateRegistrationFee() — return basePrice * 0.02 (2% registration).
4. Create two related classes in same file or separate files (prep for inheritance):
   • Car extends Vehicle — add int seatingCapacity; override or add calculateTotalCost() including registration.
   • Bike extends Vehicle — add int engineCC; different fee logic (1% instead of 2%).
5. If not extending yet, use Vehicle as standalone and note "Week 4 will add extends".
6. Demo: create one Vehicle (or Car/Bike), show info and fees.

Key concepts:
• Vehicle captures common properties all vehicles share.
• Specialization (Car vs Bike) adds unique fields.
• Registration fee varies by vehicle type — foreshadows method overriding.
• extends keyword comes fully in Week 4 — this week focus on solid base class design.
gh-w3-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: RTO systems, insurance quoting, and fleet management classify vehicles by type with shared and unique attributes. Vehicle → Car/Bike/Truck hierarchies are textbook OOP.

Compile & run:
javac Vehicle.java
java Vehicle

Push to java-week-3-oop:
git add Vehicle.java
git commit -m "Add Vehicle.java — base vehicle model"
git push

gh-w3-f8.answer:
Student Management System — menu-driven CRUD using Student class and an array of students.

Requirements:
1. Reuse or copy Student class from gh-w3-f1 (rollNumber, name, age, branch, percentage).
2. Create StudentManagementSystem.java with Student[] students (size 100), int count = 0.
3. Menu loop:
   1. Add Student (read all fields, create Student, add to array)
   2. Display All Students
   3. Search by Roll Number
   4. Display Toppers (percentage >= 90)
   5. Exit
4. Use Scanner, switch or if-else for menu.
5. Validate: no duplicate roll numbers on add.
6. Search: loop array, print match or "Not found".

Key concepts:
• CRUD: Create (add), Read (display/search), Update (optional bonus), Delete (optional bonus).
• Collection in array + count variable — same pattern as Library.
• Menu-driven apps tie Week 2 skills to OOP.
• Separate Student (entity) from StudentManagementSystem (manager) — single responsibility.
gh-w3-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: Admin panels, school ERP, and any management dashboard are CRUD over entity objects. This project is portfolio gold — it shows OOP + arrays + menus working together.

Compile & run:
javac Student.java StudentManagementSystem.java
java StudentManagementSystem

Add 3 students, search one, list toppers.

Push to java-week-3-oop:
git add Student.java StudentManagementSystem.java
git commit -m "Add StudentManagementSystem.java — CRUD menu system"
git push

gh-w3-f9.answer:
Employee Management System — parallel to Student MS but for Employee payroll records.

Requirements:
1. Reuse Employee class from gh-w3-f2 (id, name, department, basicSalary, HRA/DA/gross methods).
2. Create EmployeeManagementSystem.java with Employee[] array and count.
3. Menu:
   1. Add Employee
   2. Display All Employees
   3. Search by ID
   4. Display by Department (filter and print all in given dept)
   5. Show Highest Paid Employee
   6. Exit
4. Add: read fields, create Employee, check duplicate id.
5. Highest paid: loop all, track max gross salary.
6. Department filter: loop, compare department.equalsIgnoreCase(input).

Key concepts:
• Same management pattern as StudentMS — different entity.
• Aggregation: compute department-wise totals (optional bonus).
• Search and filter are loop + condition patterns.
• Demonstrates one codebase scaling with multiple entity types.
gh-w3-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: HR dashboards filter employees by department, find top earners, and generate reports — identical logic. Pairing StudentMS + EmployeeMS on GitHub shows you understand reusable architecture.

Compile & run:
javac Employee.java EmployeeManagementSystem.java
java EmployeeManagementSystem

Push to java-week-3-oop:
git add Employee.java EmployeeManagementSystem.java
git commit -m "Add EmployeeManagementSystem.java — employee CRUD"
git push

gh-w3-f10.answer:
README.md for java-week-3-oop — document your OOP week portfolio.

Write a README that includes:
1. Title: # java-week-3-oop
2. Description — Object-Oriented Programming in Java: classes, objects, constructors, methods, arrays of objects.
3. Topics covered — classes & objects, constructors, encapsulation, composition, CRUD systems.
4. Project structure — list entity classes (Student, Employee, Car, BankAccount, Product, Book, Vehicle) and management systems.
5. How to run — compile order matters when multiple files depend on each other:
   javac *.java
   java StudentManagementSystem
6. Class diagram (optional ASCII or mermaid) — Student → StudentManagementSystem, Book → Library.
7. What you learned — difference between class and object, why methods belong with data.
8. Link forward: "Week 4 adds inheritance, polymorphism, and larger systems."

Use tables for file listing and ```bash for commands.
gh-w3-f10.realWorld:
Why it matters: OOP week is when your GitHub stops looking like scripts and starts looking like a software project. README with entity + manager structure tells reviewers you understand real application layering.

gh-w3-f10.code:
# java-week-3-oop

Week 3 Java — Object-Oriented Programming for Prathyu Academy.

## Topics covered
- Classes, objects, constructors
- Fields and methods
- Encapsulation and validation
- Arrays of objects
- Composition (Library has Books)
- Menu-driven CRUD systems

## How to run
Requires JDK 17+.

```bash
javac *.java
java StudentManagementSystem
java EmployeeManagementSystem
```

## Files
| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| Student.java | Student entity with grade logic |
| Employee.java | Employee with payslip calculations |
| Car.java | Car with start/stop state |
| BankAccount.java | Deposit, withdraw, balance |
| Product.java | Inventory and discount pricing |
| Book.java / Library.java | Book collection management |
| Vehicle.java | Base vehicle model |
| StudentManagementSystem.java | Student CRUD menu |
| EmployeeManagementSystem.java | Employee CRUD menu |

## What I learned
I learned that a class combines data and behavior in one unit. Management systems taught me how to store multiple objects in arrays and build menu-driven CRUD apps — the same pattern used in real admin software.
